name: REPLACE: Descriptive name for this corporate strategy simulation
description: REPLACE: 2-3 sentence description of the company, its competitive landscape, and the strategic question being simulated.

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the company whose strategy is being simulated
actors.description: REPLACE: Company profile — revenue, market share, competitive position, key capabilities, and the strategic decision being evaluated.
objectives: REPLACE: Primary strategic objective (e.g., 'Grow market share from 22% to 30% within 3 years')
REPLACE: Secondary objective (e.g., 'Diversify revenue beyond core segment')
Maintain or improve profitability margins
Strengthen competitive moat against existing and new rivals
constraints: REPLACE: Capital constraint (e.g., 'Available capex budget of $X for strategic initiatives')
REPLACE: Organisational constraint (e.g., 'Integration of recent acquisition consuming management attention')
REPLACE: Market constraint (e.g., 'Regulatory approval required for expansion into adjacent segment')
Talent market tight in key technical roles
available_actions: Invest in organic growth — expand existing product lines or geographies
Pursue acquisition of competitor or adjacent player
Launch price war to gain market share
Increase R&D spending to build technology moat
Divest underperforming division to fund strategic priorities
Form strategic partnership or joint venture
Enter new market segment organically
Defend current position — optimise operations and cut costs
Lobby regulator for favourable policy treatment
Recruit key talent from competitors

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the company's primary business division (e.g., 'Retail Division')
actors.description: REPLACE: The largest or most strategically important division. Its revenue contribution, growth trajectory, and internal political weight.
objectives: REPLACE: Division-specific growth target
Protect budget allocation within the corporate portfolio
Maintain operational autonomy from corporate centre
REPLACE: Division-specific competitive objective
constraints: REPLACE: Division-specific resource limitation
Capital allocation competes with other divisions
Legacy systems or processes limit speed of change
Key customer relationships create path dependency
available_actions: Propose expansion investment to corporate board
Optimise existing operations for margin improvement
Launch competitive counter-move against rival in segment
Build case for increased budget allocation
Resist corporate-mandated changes that threaten autonomy
Collaborate with other divisions on cross-selling
Invest in customer retention programmes
Pilot new products or business models within division

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of secondary division (e.g., 'Industrial Solutions Division')
actors.description: REPLACE: A secondary division with different dynamics — perhaps smaller, faster-growing, or in a different competitive position.
objectives: REPLACE: Division-specific objective
Prove strategic value to justify continued investment
REPLACE: Growth or turnaround objective
Build capabilities that differentiate from competitors
constraints: REPLACE: Division-specific constraint
Smaller scale means less internal political influence
May face make-or-break capital allocation decision
Talent retention challenges if seen as non-core
available_actions: Pitch transformational investment to corporate board
Pursue bolt-on acquisition to accelerate growth
Form partnership with technology provider for capability leap
Restructure operations for profitability
Develop new go-to-market approach
Request increased headcount for strategic initiative
Benchmark against best-in-class competitors to build case
Accept reduced scope and focus on profitable niche

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of primary competitor
actors.description: REPLACE: The most significant competitor — market share, strategic posture (aggressive/defensive), and known strategic priorities.
objectives: REPLACE: Competitor A's primary strategic objective
Defend or grow market share against the focal firm
REPLACE: Competitor-specific growth initiative
Maintain cost leadership or differentiation advantage
constraints: REPLACE: Competitor's key weakness or constraint
REPLACE: Resource or capability limitation
Must balance offense against focal firm with other competitive threats
Shareholder expectations for consistent returns
available_actions: Launch aggressive pricing to defend market share
Invest in product innovation to differentiate
Pursue acquisition to fill capability gap
Enter new geography or segment ahead of focal firm
Poach key talent from focal firm
Form defensive alliance with other competitors
Lobby regulator to disadvantage focal firm's expansion
Increase marketing spend to strengthen brand position
Cut costs and compete on efficiency
Offer exclusive deals to focal firm's key customers

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of secondary competitor or emerging rival
actors.description: REPLACE: A secondary competitor or potential disruptor — perhaps smaller but faster-growing, or a new entrant from an adjacent sector.
objectives: REPLACE: Competitor B's primary objective (e.g., 'Disrupt the incumbent model with technology-led approach')
Capture underserved segments ignored by larger players
Achieve scale before incumbents can respond effectively
REPLACE: Specific competitive target
constraints: REPLACE: Scale limitation
REPLACE: Funding or resource constraint
Limited brand recognition compared to established players
Unproven business model at scale
available_actions: Aggressively price to acquire customers
Launch marketing campaign highlighting innovation advantage
Seek strategic partnership with larger player for distribution
Raise capital to fund growth offensive
Target focal firm's most dissatisfied customers
Develop proprietary technology as competitive moat
Expand into adjacent segments before incumbents notice
Publish thought leadership to build industry credibility

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the primary industry regulator or government body
actors.description: REPLACE: The regulatory body governing this sector — its mandate, enforcement posture, and current policy direction.
objectives: Ensure fair competition and prevent anti-competitive behaviour
Protect consumer interests and market integrity
REPLACE: Sector-specific regulatory objective
Balance innovation encouragement with risk mitigation
constraints: Legislative framework may lag market developments
Political pressure from both industry and consumer advocates
Limited enforcement resources for complex investigations
REPLACE: Sector-specific regulatory constraint
available_actions: Approve or block proposed mergers and acquisitions
Issue new regulations or guidelines
Launch investigation into competitive practices
Impose fines or corrective orders
Hold public consultations on policy changes
Grant or revoke operating licences
Publish market study or competition assessment
Coordinate with international regulatory counterparts

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of primary customer segment (e.g., 'Enterprise Clients')
actors.description: REPLACE: The most important customer segment for the focal firm — size, purchasing behaviour, loyalty, and price sensitivity.
objectives: Obtain best value (price-quality ratio) from providers
Minimise switching costs and vendor lock-in
REPLACE: Segment-specific need or objective
Ensure reliable service continuity
constraints: Switching costs create inertia (contracts, integration, habits)
Limited visibility into provider economics and true alternatives
REPLACE: Segment-specific purchasing constraint
Decision-making is distributed and slow in large organisations
available_actions: Continue current provider relationship (status quo)
Issue RFP to explore alternatives
Negotiate harder on price and terms with current provider
Switch to competitor partially or fully
Reduce overall spending in this category
Demand new features or service levels
Form buyer coalition for collective bargaining power
In-source the capability rather than buying externally

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of key supplier or upstream partner
actors.description: REPLACE: The most strategically important supplier — what they provide, concentration risk, and bargaining power dynamics.
objectives: Maximise revenue from focal firm and competitors
Maintain or increase pricing power
REPLACE: Supplier-specific strategic objective
Diversify customer base to reduce concentration risk
constraints: REPLACE: Capacity or capability constraint
Dependent on focal firm for significant revenue share
Technology cycle requires continuous investment
Competitive suppliers may undercut on price
available_actions: Maintain current terms and relationship
Increase prices citing cost pressures
Offer volume discounts for longer-term commitment
Develop exclusive product for the focal firm
Supply competitors with equivalent capabilities
Vertically integrate and compete directly
Form strategic partnership with focal firm
Reduce service levels to less profitable customers

relationships.description: Intense competitive awareness; each monitors the other's moves closely
Emerging competitive threat; focal firm may underestimate this player
Core revenue relationship; customer loyalty is the key strategic asset
Competitor A also serves this segment; overlap creates switching opportunity
Emerging competitor targeting the segment with differentiated offering
Regulatory authority over the focal firm's operations and market behaviour
Same regulatory framework applies to all market participants
Newer entrant may face lighter or heavier regulatory scrutiny depending on framework
Critical input provider; relationship health affects focal firm's cost structure and capabilities
Supplier also serves competitor; potential information leakage or preferential treatment concern
Primary division; high internal influence and budget share
Secondary division; competes internally for attention and resources
Internal rivalry for capital allocation and management attention
Incumbents monitor each other and emerging disruptors

prediction_questions: Will the focal firm gain or lose market share by round 50?
Which competitor will make the most aggressive strategic move first?
Will the focal firm pursue an acquisition during the simulation?
Will the regulator take any action that significantly alters competitive dynamics?
Will competitor B emerge as a serious threat to the focal firm?
Which division of the focal firm will receive the largest capital allocation?
Will the key supplier exercise pricing power during the simulation?
Will any unexpected alliance form between competitors?
